name: types
context: TypeScript type system — primitives, unions, intersections, narrowing, utility types, conditional types, and type-level programming
info: The TypeScript type system is structural and gradual: types are compared by shape (not name), and you can opt out with `any` or opt in with `strict: true`. This node covers every layer of the type system that an agent needs to write and understand TypeScript: primitive types, literal types, union and intersection types, type narrowing with control flow analysis, type guards, discriminated unions, tuple types, template literal types, conditional types, mapped types, indexed access types, and the full set of built-in utility types. Understanding the type system is essential for reading TypeScript error messages, writing correct generic functions, and building type-safe APIs. See [[typescript/generics]] for parameterized types and [[typescript/config]] for strict mode options that affect type checking.

sections.title: Primitive types
sections.body: TypeScript has 7 primitive types: `string`, `number`, `bigint`, `boolean`, `symbol`, `undefined`, `null`. Plus special types: `void` (functions returning nothing), `never` (functions that never return — throws or infinite loops), `any` (opt out of type checking), `unknown` (type-safe catch-all — must narrow before use). Always prefer `unknown` over `any` for values of uncertain type. Use `void` for function return types, not `undefined`. The `object` type means any non-primitive; prefer specific interfaces.

sections.title: Type narrowing and control flow analysis
sections.body: TypeScript narrows types through control flow analysis. Narrowing techniques: `typeof` checks (`typeof x === "string"`), `instanceof` checks (`x instanceof Date`), `in` operator (`"swim" in animal`), truthiness (`if (x)`), equality (`x === null`), assignment (`x = "hello"`), and custom type guards (`function isFish(x): x is Fish`). After a narrowing check, TypeScript knows the more specific type in that branch. Discriminated unions use a literal property as a discriminant — switch on it for exhaustive handling. TypeScript 5.5+ can infer type predicates from function bodies automatically.

sections.title: Built-in utility types
sections.body: TypeScript ships with utility types for common transformations. Object: `Partial<T>` (all optional), `Required<T>` (all required), `Readonly<T>` (all readonly), `Pick<T, K>` (select properties), `Omit<T, K>` (remove properties), `Record<K, V>` (key-value map). Union: `Exclude<T, U>` (remove from union), `Extract<T, U>` (keep from union), `NonNullable<T>` (remove null/undefined). Function: `ReturnType<T>`, `Parameters<T>`, `ConstructorParameters<T>`, `InstanceType<T>`. Promise: `Awaited<T>` (unwrap promise). String: `Uppercase<T>`, `Lowercase<T>`, `Capitalize<T>`, `Uncapitalize<T>`. New: `NoInfer<T>` (5.4+) prevents inference.

sections.title: Conditional and mapped types
sections.body: Conditional types branch on type relationships: `T extends U ? X : Y`. They distribute over unions by default — `ToArray<string | number>` becomes `string[] | number[]`. Use `[T] extends [U]` to prevent distribution. The `infer` keyword extracts types: `T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T` unwraps promises. Mapped types transform property types: `{ [K in keyof T]: V }`. The `as` clause remaps keys: `{ [K in keyof T as NewKey]: T[K] }`. Modifiers `+`/`-` add or remove `readonly` and `?`: `{ -readonly [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] }` makes all properties mutable and required.

sections.title: Type assertions and const assertions
sections.body: Type assertions tell TypeScript you know better: `value as Type`. Use sparingly — they bypass type safety. The `as const` assertion makes values deeply readonly with literal types — essential for deriving union types from arrays and object values. The `satisfies` operator (4.9+) validates a value matches a type without widening — `const x = { ... } satisfies Schema` keeps literal types while ensuring the shape matches. The non-null assertion `x!` tells TypeScript `x` is not null/undefined — prefer proper null checking instead. Double assertion `x as unknown as T` is a code smell but sometimes necessary for framework integration.
